subtopics.name: Singleton Pattern
subtopics.content:
Singleton Pattern

The Singleton Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that ensures only one instance of a class exists throughout the application and provides a global access point to that instance.

Why Do We Need Singleton?

Some resources should have only one instance in the entire application.

Creating multiple instances may lead to inconsistent behavior, unnecessary memory usage, or resource conflicts.

Examples

  • Database Connection Manager
  • Logger
  • Configuration Manager
  • Cache Manager
  • Thread Pool Manager

Imagine every service creating its own database connection manager. 


Service A -> DB Manager 1

Service B -> DB Manager 2

Service C -> DB Manager 3

This leads to wasted resources and management complexity.

Instead: 


Service A
      \
       \
Service B -----> Singleton DB Manager
       /
      /
Service C

Problem Without Singleton


DatabaseManager db1 = new DatabaseManager();

DatabaseManager db2 = new DatabaseManager();

DatabaseManager db3 = new DatabaseManager();

Now three different objects exist.

This may create:

  • Memory wastage.
  • Resource contention.
  • Data inconsistency.

Singleton Solution

The Singleton Pattern restricts object creation by:

  1. Making the constructor private.
  2. Creating a static instance.
  3. Providing a public method to access the instance.

Basic Singleton Implementation


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Usage


Singleton s1 = Singleton.getInstance();

Singleton s2 = Singleton.getInstance();

System.out.println(s1 == s2);

Output: 


true

Both references point to the same object.

How It Works


First Call

getInstance()

instance == null

Create Object

Return Object

Subsequent calls: 


getInstance()

instance != null

Return Existing Object

Characteristics of Singleton

  • Only one object exists.
  • Global access point.
  • Object created on demand.
  • Controlled object lifecycle.

Eager Initialization Singleton

The object is created when the class is loaded. 


public class Singleton {

    private static final Singleton instance =
            new Singleton();

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {
        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Simple implementation.

Disadvantages

  • Object created even if not used.
  • Memory wastage possible.

Lazy Initialization Singleton

Object is created only when required. 


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Saves memory.
  • Object created only when needed.

Disadvantages

  • Not thread safe.

Thread Safety Problem

Suppose two threads call getInstance() simultaneously. 


Thread 1 -> instance == null

Thread 2 -> instance == null

Both threads create separate objects. 


Singleton Object 1

Singleton Object 2

Singleton guarantee is broken.

Thread Safe Singleton


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static synchronized Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Simple implementation.

Disadvantages

  • Performance overhead due to synchronization.

Double Checked Locking

This is the most common interview implementation. 


public class Singleton {

    private static volatile Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {

            synchronized (Singleton.class) {

                if(instance == null) {
                    instance = new Singleton();
                }
            }
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • High performance.
  • Object created only when required.

Best Singleton Implementation (Enum)

Joshua Bloch recommends using Enum Singleton. 


public enum Singleton {

    INSTANCE;

    public void show() {
        System.out.println("Singleton");
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Serialization safe.
  • Reflection safe.
  • Simplest implementation.

Real World Examples

Logger


Logger logger = Logger.getInstance();

All application components use the same logger instance.

Configuration Manager


ConfigManager.getInstance();

Single source of configuration data.

Cache Manager


CacheManager.getInstance();

Shared cache across the application.

Singleton in Spring Framework

By default, Spring Beans are Singleton scoped. 


@Service
public class UserService {
}

Spring creates only one instance of UserService and shares it across the application.

Advantages

  • Controlled object creation.
  • Memory efficient.
  • Global access point.
  • Useful for shared resources.
  • Easy lifecycle management.

Disadvantages

  • Acts like a global variable.
  • Difficult to unit test.
  • Can introduce hidden dependencies.
  • May violate Single Responsibility Principle.
  • Thread safety must be handled carefully.

Common Interview Questions

Why is Singleton a Creational Pattern?

Because it controls how objects are created.

How do you make Singleton thread safe?

Using synchronization, double-checked locking, or Enum.

What is the best Singleton implementation in Java?

Enum Singleton is generally considered the safest implementation.

Are Spring Beans Singleton?

Yes. Singleton is the default bean scope in Spring.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Singleton ensures only one object exists.
  • Private constructor prevents external object creation.
  • Global access is provided through getInstance().
  • Lazy initialization creates object on demand.
  • Double Checked Locking is a common production implementation.
  • Enum Singleton is considered the safest Java implementation.
  • Spring Beans are Singleton by default.

subtopics.name: Factory Method Pattern
subtopics.content:
Factory Method Pattern

The Factory Method Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that provides an interface for creating objects while allowing subclasses to decide which specific object to instantiate.

Why Do We Need Factory Method?

Direct object creation using the new keyword tightly couples the client code with concrete classes.

Whenever a new implementation is introduced, client code must be modified. 


Payment payment = new CreditCardPayment();

If tomorrow PayPalPayment is introduced, existing code needs modification.

Factory Method solves this by moving object creation logic into a dedicated factory.

Problem Without Factory Method


if(type.equals("CREDIT_CARD")) {
    payment = new CreditCardPayment();
}
else if(type.equals("UPI")) {
    payment = new UpiPayment();
}
else if(type.equals("PAYPAL")) {
    payment = new PaypalPayment();
}

Every new payment method requires code changes.

Factory Method Solution

Instead of creating objects directly, the client asks a factory to create them. 


Client
   |
   v
Factory
   |
   +--> CreditCardPayment
   |
   +--> UpiPayment
   |
   +--> PaypalPayment

Implementation

Step 1: Create Product Interface


public interface Payment {

    void pay();
}

Step 2: Concrete Products


public class CreditCardPayment
        implements Payment {

    @Override
    public void pay() {
        System.out.println(
            "Credit Card Payment");
    }
}


public class UpiPayment
        implements Payment {

    @Override
    public void pay() {
        System.out.println(
            "UPI Payment");
    }
}

Step 3: Create Factory


public class PaymentFactory {

    public static Payment getPayment(
            String type) {

        if(type.equals("CARD")) {
            return new CreditCardPayment();
        }

        if(type.equals("UPI")) {
            return new UpiPayment();
        }

        throw new IllegalArgumentException();
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Payment payment =
    PaymentFactory.getPayment("UPI");

payment.pay();

How It Works

  1. Client requests an object.
  2. Factory decides which implementation to create.
  3. Factory returns the object.
  4. Client uses the interface.

Client remains unaware of concrete implementations.

Real World Example

Consider a logistics company. 


Road Transport
Air Transport
Sea Transport

The client asks for a transport service.

The factory determines which transport implementation to create.

Factory Method vs Direct Object Creation

Direct Creation Factory Method
Tightly Coupled Loosely Coupled
Hard to Extend Easy to Extend
Client Knows Class Client Knows Interface

Factory Method in Spring

Spring uses Factory Method extensively. 


@Bean
public PaymentService paymentService() {
    return new PaymentServiceImpl();
}

The framework calls the factory method to create the bean.

Advantages

  • Reduces tight coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Centralized object creation.
  • Easier maintenance.
  • Improves scalability.

Disadvantages

  • Additional classes required.
  • Increased complexity for simple applications.
  • More abstraction layers.

When to Use

  • Multiple implementations exist.
  • Object creation logic is complex.
  • Client should not know concrete classes.
  • Future extensions are expected.

When Not to Use

  • Only one implementation exists.
  • Object creation is extremely simple.
  • Abstraction adds unnecessary complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Factory Method solve?

It decouples object creation from client code.

Which SOLID principle does it support?

Open/Closed Principle and Dependency Inversion Principle.

How is it different from Abstract Factory?

Factory Method creates one product type, whereas Abstract Factory creates families of related products.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Factory Method is a Creational Design Pattern.
  • Object creation is delegated to a factory.
  • Reduces tight coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Client depends on abstraction, not implementation.
  • Commonly used in Spring Bean creation.

subtopics.name: Abstract Factory Pattern
subtopics.content:
Abstract Factory Pattern

The Abstract Factory Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that provides an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.

Why Do We Need Abstract Factory?

In Factory Method Pattern, we create one object at a time.

However, many applications require creating multiple related objects that belong to the same family.

For example:

  • Windows Button + Windows Checkbox
  • Mac Button + Mac Checkbox
  • Linux Button + Linux Checkbox

We should ensure that related objects belong to the same family.

Problem Without Abstract Factory


Button button =
    new WindowsButton();

Checkbox checkbox =
    new MacCheckbox();

Now the UI contains components from different operating systems.

This creates inconsistency.

Abstract Factory Solution

The Abstract Factory creates an entire family of related objects. 


Client
   |
   v
GUI Factory
   |
   +--> Button
   |
   +--> Checkbox

Depending on the selected factory: 


Windows Factory
    |
    +--> Windows Button
    |
    +--> Windows Checkbox

Mac Factory
    |
    +--> Mac Button
    |
    +--> Mac Checkbox

Implementation

Step 1: Create Product Interfaces


public interface Button {
    void paint();
}

public interface Checkbox {
    void check();
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Products


public class WindowsButton
        implements Button {

    public void paint() {
        System.out.println(
          "Windows Button");
    }
}


public class WindowsCheckbox
        implements Checkbox {

    public void check() {
        System.out.println(
          "Windows Checkbox");
    }
}


public class MacButton
        implements Button {

    public void paint() {
        System.out.println(
          "Mac Button");
    }
}


public class MacCheckbox
        implements Checkbox {

    public void check() {
        System.out.println(
          "Mac Checkbox");
    }
}

Step 3: Create Abstract Factory


public interface GUIFactory {

    Button createButton();

    Checkbox createCheckbox();
}

Step 4: Create Concrete Factories


public class WindowsFactory
        implements GUIFactory {

    public Button createButton() {
        return new WindowsButton();
    }

    public Checkbox createCheckbox() {
        return new WindowsCheckbox();
    }
}


public class MacFactory
        implements GUIFactory {

    public Button createButton() {
        return new MacButton();
    }

    public Checkbox createCheckbox() {
        return new MacCheckbox();
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


GUIFactory factory =
    new WindowsFactory();

Button button =
    factory.createButton();

Checkbox checkbox =
    factory.createCheckbox();

button.paint();
checkbox.check();

How It Works

  1. Client selects a factory.
  2. Factory creates related products.
  3. Client works with interfaces.
  4. Concrete implementations remain hidden.

Factory Method vs Abstract Factory

Factory Method Abstract Factory
Creates one product Creates product families
Single factory method Multiple factory methods
Simpler More complex
One object creation Group object creation

Real World Example

Imagine a car manufacturing company.

Different factories produce different vehicle families. 


Toyota Factory

Toyota Car
Toyota Engine
Toyota Dashboard


BMW Factory

BMW Car
BMW Engine
BMW Dashboard

The client simply chooses a factory.

Abstract Factory in Spring

Spring's BeanFactory and ApplicationContext are examples of Abstract Factory concepts. 


ApplicationContext context =
    new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext();

UserService service =
    context.getBean(UserService.class);

The framework decides which implementation to provide.

Advantages

  • Ensures compatibility between products.
  • Reduces coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Centralized object creation.
  • Easy to switch product families.

Disadvantages

  • Many classes and interfaces required.
  • Complex design.
  • Difficult to add new product types.

When to Use

  • Multiple product families exist.
  • Products must work together.
  • Need to switch entire families dynamically.
  • Object creation should be hidden.

When Not to Use

  • Only one product exists.
  • No related object families exist.
  • Simple applications.

Common Interview Questions

What is the difference between Factory Method and Abstract Factory?

Factory Method creates one object. Abstract Factory creates families of related objects.

Which SOLID principle does it support?

Open/Closed Principle and Dependency Inversion Principle.

Where is it used in Spring?

BeanFactory and ApplicationContext follow Abstract Factory concepts.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Abstract Factory creates families of related objects.
  • Client depends on interfaces, not implementations.
  • Ensures product compatibility.
  • More powerful than Factory Method.
  • Commonly used in UI frameworks.
  • Spring BeanFactory is a practical example.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: DNS Overview
subtopics.content: The Domain Name System (DNS) is a hierarchical naming system that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses, enabling browsers to locate and communicate with web servers.

subtopics.name: DNS Resolver
subtopics.content: A DNS Resolver, also known as a Recursive Resolver, receives DNS queries from clients and performs the lookup process on their behalf. It communicates with Root Servers, TLD Servers, and Authoritative Name Servers to resolve a domain name into an IP address.

subtopics.name: Root DNS Server
subtopics.content: Root DNS Servers are the first step in DNS resolution. They do not store the IP address of a domain but direct the DNS Resolver to the appropriate Top-Level Domain (TLD) server based on the domain extension such as .com, .org, or .net.

subtopics.name: Top-Level Domain (TLD) Server
subtopics.content: TLD Servers manage information about domains within a specific top-level domain, such as .com or .org. They provide the DNS Resolver with the address of the Authoritative Name Server responsible for the requested domain.

subtopics.name: Authoritative Name Server
subtopics.content: An Authoritative Name Server stores the actual DNS records for a domain. When queried, it returns the requested information, such as the IP address associated with a domain name.

subtopics.name: DNS Resolution Process
subtopics.content: When a user enters a domain name such as telusko.com, the DNS Resolver first queries a Root DNS Server. The Root Server responds with the location of the appropriate TLD Server (for example, .com). The Resolver then queries the TLD Server, which returns the address of the Authoritative Name Server. Finally, the Resolver queries the Authoritative Name Server and receives the domain's IP address, which is returned to the user's browser.

subtopics.name: DNS Caching
subtopics.content: DNS Caching stores previously resolved domain names and their IP addresses for a specified Time To Live (TTL). This reduces lookup time and minimizes the number of requests sent to DNS servers.

subtopics.name: DNS Records
subtopics.content: DNS Records are entries stored in DNS servers that provide information about a domain. Common record types include A Records (IPv4 addresses), AAAA Records (IPv6 addresses), CNAME Records (aliases), MX Records (mail servers), and TXT Records (text-based information).

subtopics.name: What is Caching?
subtopics.content: Caching is a technique used to store frequently accessed data temporarily so that future requests can be served faster. Think of it like keeping your favorite snacks close by instead of going to the store every time.

subtopics.name: Why Caching is Important
subtopics.content: Caching improves performance and reduces load on databases or servers. By reusing stored data, applications respond faster and handle more users efficiently.

subtopics.name: Types of Caching
subtopics.content:
There are several types of caching:

  • Memory Cache: Data stored in RAM for quick access.
  • Disk Cache: Data stored on disk to persist between sessions.
  • Browser Cache: Stores web content locally to speed up page loading.
  • Application Cache: Used by software applications to store computation results.

subtopics.name: Cache Expiration and TTL
subtopics.content: Cached data is not permanent. Each item has a Time To Live (TTL) or expiration time. Once expired, the cache must fetch fresh data from the source.

subtopics.name: Cache Invalidation
subtopics.content: Cache invalidation is the process of removing outdated or incorrect data from the cache. It ensures users always get the most accurate and up-to-date information.

subtopics.name: Cache Best Practices
subtopics.content:
Some best practices include:

  • Only cache frequently accessed data.
  • Set appropriate TTL values to balance freshness and performance.
  • Use distributed caches for large applications.
  • Monitor cache hit/miss rates to optimize performance.

subtopics.name: Real-World Examples
subtopics.content: Examples of caching include web browser cache for images, CDNs caching static website content, Redis or Memcached storing session data, and CPU cache storing instructions for faster processing.

subtopics.name: Read Through Cache (RTC)
subtopics.content: Read Through Cache is a caching strategy where the cache sits between the application and the database. The application always queries the cache first. If the data is not present, the cache itself fetches it from the database, stores it, and returns it to the application.

subtopics.name: Cache Hit in Read Through Cache
subtopics.content:
In a cache hit scenario, the requested data is already present in the cache. The cache directly returns the data to the application without contacting the database, resulting in very fast response times.

  • Application → Cache → Response

subtopics.name: Cache Miss in Read Through Cache
subtopics.content:
In a cache miss scenario, the cache does not contain the requested data. The cache fetches the data from the database, stores it internally, and then returns it to the application.

  • Application → Cache → Database → Cache → Application

subtopics.name: Write Through Cache (WTC)
subtopics.content: Write Through Cache is a strategy where every write operation is first written to the cache and then immediately written to the database. This ensures strong consistency between cache and database.

subtopics.name: Write Through Cache Flow
subtopics.content:
In Write Through Cache, when data is updated, it is written simultaneously to both cache and database. This ensures the cache always stays in sync with the database but may introduce higher write latency.

  • Client → Backend → Cache → Database

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache (Cache Aside Pattern)
subtopics.content: Write Aside Cache is a strategy where the application writes directly to the database and then updates or invalidates the cache separately. It is the most commonly used caching pattern in real-world systems.

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache Read Flow
subtopics.content:
On a cache hit, data is returned directly from cache. On a cache miss, the application fetches data from the database, stores it in cache, and returns it to the user.

  • Cache Hit: Client → Backend → Cache → Response
  • Cache Miss: Client → Backend → Cache → DB → Cache → Response

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache Write Flow
subtopics.content:
In Write Aside Cache, write operations update the database first. After a successful update, the cache is either invalidated or refreshed to avoid stale data.

  • Client → Backend → Database → Cache Invalidate

subtopics.name: Comparison of RTC, WTC, and WAC
subtopics.content:
• Read Through Cache: Cache handles DB fetch on miss.
  • Write Through Cache: Writes go to cache and DB simultaneously.
  • Write Aside Cache: App writes to DB first, cache updated later.

Write Aside Cache is the most widely used in modern backend systems due to its simplicity and scalability.

subtopics.name: When to Use Each Cache Strategy
subtopics.content:
• RTC: When you want cache-managed data loading.
  • WTC: When strong consistency is required.
  • WAC: When read-heavy systems need scalability and flexibility.

subtopics.name: Common  Questions
subtopics.content:
1. What is Read Through Cache?
  2. What is Write Through Cache?
  3. What is Write Aside Cache?
  4. Difference between RTC and WAC?
  5. Difference between WTC and WAC?
  6. Which caching strategy is most used in production?
  7. How does cache invalidation work in WAC?
  8. What are the risks of cache stampede?
  9. When would you avoid Write Through Cache?
  10. How do these caching strategies affect system performance?

subtopics.name: Least Recently Used (LRU)
subtopics.content: Evicts the item that has not been used for the longest time. Good for workloads where recently used items are likely to be reused.

subtopics.name: Least Frequently Used (LFU)
subtopics.content: Evicts the item that has been used the fewest times. Useful when some items are accessed more frequently than others.

subtopics.name: First In, First Out (FIFO)
subtopics.content: Evicts the oldest item in the cache. Simple to implement but may remove frequently accessed items.

subtopics.name: Random Replacement
subtopics.content: Evicts a random item. Simple and occasionally effective, especially in distributed caches where tracking LRU/LFU is expensive.

subtopics.name: Time-To-Live (TTL) / Expiration-Based
subtopics.content: Each cached item has a lifetime (TTL). Once expired, it is removed automatically. Useful for periodically changing data.

subtopics.name: Adaptive Replacement Cache (ARC)
subtopics.content: Combines LRU and LFU principles. Maintains two lists: recently used and frequently used, providing a better hit rate for mixed workloads.

subtopics.name: Segmented LRU (SLRU)
subtopics.content: Divides the cache into probationary and protected segments. Items move between segments based on usage patterns. Useful in high-performance caching systems.

subtopics.name: Most Recently Used (MRU)
subtopics.content: Evicts the most recently used item. Useful in sequential access patterns where the most recently accessed items are unlikely to be used again soon. Common in database buffer pools or sequential scans.

subtopics.name: Basics of Load Balancer
subtopics.content:
Load Balancer

A Load Balancer (LB) is a component that distributes incoming requests across multiple servers to improve availability, scalability, and reliability of an application.

Why Do We Need a Load Balancer?

Imagine an application running on a single server. As the number of users grows, the server may become overloaded and unable to handle all requests efficiently.

A load balancer sits between clients and application servers and distributes traffic among multiple servers, ensuring no single server becomes a bottleneck.

How It Works

  1. User enters a domain name in the browser.
  2. DNS resolves the domain name to the Load Balancer's IP address.
  3. The request reaches the Load Balancer.
  4. The Load Balancer selects an available server.
  5. The selected server processes the request and returns the response.

User
  |
  v
 DNS
  |
  v
Load Balancer
  |
  +----> Server 1
  |
  +----> Server 2
  |
  +----> Server 3

Main Responsibilities of a Load Balancer

1. Choosing the Server

The primary responsibility of a load balancer is deciding which server should handle the incoming request.

Common algorithms include:

  • Round Robin – Requests are distributed sequentially among servers.
  • Weighted Round Robin – Servers with higher capacity receive more requests.
  • Least Connections – Request goes to the server with the fewest active connections.
  • IP Hash – Same client IP is routed to the same server.

2. Health Checks

A load balancer continuously monitors the health of backend servers.

If a server becomes unavailable or unhealthy, the load balancer stops sending requests to that server and redirects traffic to healthy servers.

Example:


Server 1 -> Healthy ✓
Server 2 -> Healthy ✓
Server 3 -> Down ✗

New requests will only be sent to Server 1 and Server 2.

Benefits of Load Balancer

  • Improves application availability.
  • Distributes traffic evenly.
  • Supports horizontal scaling.
  • Provides fault tolerance.
  • Reduces server overload.
  • Improves response time.

Types of Load Balancers

Type Layer Description
Layer 4 Transport Layer Routes traffic using IP address and port.
Layer 7 Application Layer Routes traffic based on URL, headers, cookies, etc.

Real World Example

Suppose an e-commerce website receives 100,000 users during a sale. Instead of running the application on a single server, multiple application servers are deployed. 


Customers
    |
    v
Load Balancer
    |
+---+---+---+
|   |   |   |
S1  S2  S3  S4

The Load Balancer distributes requests among all servers, allowing the application to handle heavy traffic without downtime.

Quick Notes

  • Load Balancer distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers.
  • Improves scalability, availability, and fault tolerance.
  • Performs health checks on backend servers.
  • Common algorithms: Round Robin, Weighted Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash.
  • Layer 4 works on IP and Port.
  • Layer 7 works on HTTP attributes like URL, Headers, and Cookies.
  • Examples: Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ELB, Google Cloud Load Balancer.

subtopics.name: Algorithms of Load Balancer
subtopics.content:
Load Balancing Algorithms

A Load Balancer receives incoming requests and decides which backend server should handle them. The decision-making process is based on a load balancing algorithm.

Different algorithms are suitable for different use cases. Choosing the wrong algorithm can lead to uneven traffic distribution, poor response times, and resource wastage.

1. Round Robin

Round Robin is the simplest and most commonly used load balancing algorithm.

Requests are distributed sequentially among available servers. 


Request 1 -> Server 1
Request 2 -> Server 2
Request 3 -> Server 3
Request 4 -> Server 1
Request 5 -> Server 2

Advantages

  • Simple to implement.
  • Very low overhead.
  • Works well when all servers have identical capacity.
  • Traffic distribution is predictable.

Disadvantages

  • Assumes all servers have equal resources.
  • Does not consider server load.
  • Long-running requests may overload some servers.
  • Poor choice when server capacities differ.

Best Use Case

Applications where all servers have similar hardware and traffic patterns.

2. Geo-Based Algorithm

Geo-based load balancing routes users to the nearest server based on geographical location. 


India User  -> Mumbai Server
US User     -> Virginia Server
Europe User -> Frankfurt Server

Location can be determined using IP geolocation.

Advantages

  • Reduces network latency.
  • Improves user experience.
  • Reduces cross-region traffic costs.
  • Supports disaster recovery across regions.

Disadvantages

  • Requires geographically distributed infrastructure.
  • IP geolocation may be inaccurate.
  • Nearby server may still be overloaded.
  • More complex to configure.

Best Use Case

Global applications such as Netflix, Amazon, Google, and Cloudflare.

3. Least Connections Algorithm

The load balancer sends the next request to the server having the fewest active connections. 


Server 1 -> 50 active connections
Server 2 -> 20 active connections
Server 3 -> 10 active connections

Next Request -> Server 3

Advantages

  • Considers current server load.
  • Works well for long-lived connections.
  • Provides better balancing than Round Robin.
  • Reduces chances of server overload.

Disadvantages

  • Requires continuous monitoring of active connections.
  • Connection count may not represent actual resource usage.
  • Slightly higher overhead.

Best Use Case

WebSocket applications, chat applications, and streaming systems.

4. Least Response Time Algorithm

Requests are routed to the server with the fastest response time.

The load balancer continuously measures response times and chooses the fastest server. 


Server 1 -> 120 ms
Server 2 -> 80 ms
Server 3 -> 50 ms

Next Request -> Server 3

Advantages

  • Provides optimal user experience.
  • Automatically adapts to changing server performance.
  • Helps avoid slow servers.
  • Improves overall response time.

Disadvantages

  • Requires continuous monitoring.
  • Higher computational overhead.
  • Response times may fluctuate rapidly.
  • More complex than Round Robin.

Best Use Case

High-performance systems where latency is critical.

5. IP Hash Algorithm

The client's IP address is hashed to determine the destination server. 


hash(Client IP) % Number of Servers

A specific user is consistently routed to the same server. 


192.168.1.10 -> Server 2
192.168.1.10 -> Server 2
192.168.1.10 -> Server 2

Advantages

  • Provides session persistence.
  • Simple implementation.
  • Useful when session data is stored locally.
  • Reduces need for distributed session storage.

Disadvantages

  • Uneven traffic distribution may occur.
  • Adding/removing servers changes hash mappings.
  • Some servers may become overloaded.
  • Not ideal for dynamic environments.

Best Use Case

Legacy applications that depend on sticky sessions.

6. Weighted Round Robin

Weighted Round Robin is an enhancement of Round Robin where servers receive traffic according to their assigned weights. 


Server 1 Weight = 5
Server 2 Weight = 3
Server 3 Weight = 2

Traffic Distribution:

S1 S1 S1 S1 S1
S2 S2 S2
S3 S3

A more powerful server receives more requests.

Advantages

  • Handles heterogeneous infrastructure.
  • Better utilization of powerful servers.
  • Simple implementation.
  • More balanced than basic Round Robin.

Disadvantages

  • Weights must be configured manually.
  • Cannot react to real-time server load.
  • Incorrect weights can create imbalance.

Best Use Case

Clusters where servers have different CPU, RAM, or hardware capacities.

Hybrid Approach

Modern load balancers rarely rely on a single algorithm. Instead, they combine multiple algorithms to make smarter routing decisions.

Example 1: Geo + Least Connections


Step 1: Route user to nearest region.
Step 2: Select server with least connections.

Used by global cloud providers.

Example 2: Weighted Round Robin + Health Checks


Server 1 Weight = 5
Server 2 Weight = 3
Server 3 Weight = 2

If Server 1 becomes unhealthy,
all traffic is redistributed to healthy servers.

Example 3: IP Hash + Least Response Time

Maintain session affinity while preferring servers with better performance.

Advantages of Hybrid Approach

  • More intelligent routing.
  • Better scalability.
  • Improved fault tolerance.
  • Optimized performance and latency.
  • Can adapt to changing traffic conditions.

Disadvantages of Hybrid Approach

  • More complex configuration.
  • Higher monitoring overhead.
  • Requires advanced load balancers.

Comparison Table

Algorithm Considers Load Session Persistence Complexity
Round Robin No No Low
Weighted Round Robin Partially No Low
Least Connections Yes No Medium
Least Response Time Yes No High
IP Hash No Yes Medium
Geo Based Region Level Optional High

Summary

  • Round Robin is the simplest and most commonly used algorithm.
  • Weighted Round Robin is suitable when servers have different capacities.
  • Least Connections is preferred for long-lived connections.
  • Least Response Time focuses on latency optimization.
  • IP Hash provides sticky sessions.
  • Geo-Based Routing improves global user experience.
  • Modern cloud systems commonly use hybrid load balancing strategies.

subtopics.name: Health Checks in Load Balancer
subtopics.content:
Health Checks in Load Balancers

A Load Balancer continuously monitors the health of backend servers to ensure traffic is only routed to healthy instances.

Without health checks, requests may be sent to failed or overloaded servers, resulting in application downtime and poor user experience.

Why Are Health Checks Needed?

Consider a system with three application servers. 


Load Balancer
     |
 +---+---+---+
 |   |   |   |
S1  S2  S3

If Server S2 crashes but the Load Balancer is unaware of the failure, it will continue sending requests to S2, causing request failures.

Health checks allow the Load Balancer to automatically detect unhealthy servers and remove them from the traffic pool.

How Health Checks Work

  1. Load Balancer periodically sends health check requests.
  2. Server responds with success or failure.
  3. Load Balancer tracks consecutive successes and failures.
  4. If failure threshold is reached, the server is marked unhealthy.
  5. If recovery threshold is reached, the server is marked healthy again.

Health Check Request
        |
        v
     Server
        |
        +--> Success -> Healthy
        |
        +--> Failure -> Unhealthy

Health Check Parameters

1. Interval

Interval defines how often the Load Balancer performs health checks.

Example: 


Interval = 5 seconds

This means every 5 seconds the Load Balancer sends a health check request to each backend server.

Advantages of Small Interval

  • Detects failures quickly.
  • Faster traffic rerouting.
  • Improves availability.

Disadvantages of Small Interval

  • Increased network traffic.
  • Higher monitoring overhead.
  • Additional load on backend servers.

Example


Interval = 5 seconds

00:00 -> Check
00:05 -> Check
00:10 -> Check
00:15 -> Check

2. Timeout

Timeout defines how long the Load Balancer waits for a response from the server.

If the server does not respond within the timeout period, the health check is considered failed. 


Timeout = 2 seconds

Request Sent
      |
      +--> Response within 2 sec -> Success
      |
      +--> No Response after 2 sec -> Failure

Example

Response Time Result
500 ms Pass
1.5 sec Pass
3 sec Fail

Choosing Timeout

  • Too small → healthy servers may be marked unhealthy.
  • Too large → failure detection becomes slow.
  • Usually smaller than the interval.

3. Threshold

Threshold determines how many consecutive successes or failures are required before changing the server's health status.

Unhealthy Threshold

Number of consecutive failed health checks required before marking a server unhealthy. 


Unhealthy Threshold = 3

Check 1 -> Fail
Check 2 -> Fail
Check 3 -> Fail

Result:
Server marked UNHEALTHY

This prevents temporary network glitches from immediately removing a server.

Healthy Threshold

Number of consecutive successful health checks required before bringing a server back into service. 


Healthy Threshold = 2

Check 1 -> Success
Check 2 -> Success

Result:
Server marked HEALTHY

This prevents unstable servers from repeatedly entering and leaving the traffic pool.

Real World Example

Assume the following configuration: 


Interval = 5 sec
Timeout = 2 sec
Unhealthy Threshold = 3
Healthy Threshold = 2

Server crashes at 10:00:00. 


10:00:05 -> Fail (1)
10:00:10 -> Fail (2)
10:00:15 -> Fail (3)

Server marked UNHEALTHY

Server recovers later. 


10:01:00 -> Success (1)
10:01:05 -> Success (2)

Server marked HEALTHY

Types of Health Checks

TCP Health Check

Checks whether a TCP connection can be established. 


Can connect to port 8080?
YES -> Healthy
NO  -> Unhealthy

HTTP Health Check

Calls a dedicated endpoint such as: 


GET /health
GET /actuator/health

Server returns HTTP 200 if healthy.

HTTPS Health Check

Same as HTTP health checks but over SSL/TLS.

Custom Health Check

Application verifies database, cache, message queues, and other dependencies before reporting healthy status.

Spring Boot Example

Spring Boot Actuator provides a built-in health endpoint. 


implementation 'org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-actuator'

Health endpoint: 


GET /actuator/health

Typical response: 


{
  "status": "UP"
}

Quick Notes

  • Health checks ensure traffic is sent only to healthy servers.
  • Interval defines how often health checks run.
  • Timeout defines how long the Load Balancer waits for a response.
  • Unhealthy Threshold defines failures required to mark a server down.
  • Healthy Threshold defines successes required to mark a server healthy.
  • TCP checks verify connectivity.
  • HTTP checks verify application availability.
  • Spring Boot commonly exposes /actuator/health for Load Balancer monitoring.

subtopics.name: Basics of database replication
subtopics.content:
Database Replication

Database Replication is the process of maintaining multiple copies of the same data across different database servers.

Whenever data is written to the primary database, the changes are propagated to one or more replica databases to keep them synchronized.

Why Do We Need Database Replication?

As applications grow, a single database server becomes a bottleneck for availability, performance, and scalability.

Replication helps solve these problems by creating additional copies of data.

1. Eliminate Single Point of Failure (SPOF)

If all data exists on only one database server and that server crashes, the entire application may become unavailable. 


Application
     |
     v
 Database

Database Down = Application Down

With replication, another database copy can take over. 


Application
     |
     v
 Primary DB
     |
     v
 Replica DB

Primary Down -> Replica Takes Over

2. Improve Availability

Since multiple copies of data exist, users can continue accessing the application even if one database server becomes unavailable.

This significantly improves system uptime.

3. Improve Performance

Read requests can be distributed across multiple replica databases.

Instead of a single database handling all traffic, the workload is shared. 


            Read Requests
                 |
        +--------+--------+
        |        |        |
      Replica1 Replica2 Replica3

            Writes
              |
           Primary

This reduces load on the primary database.

4. Keep Data Close to Users

Organizations often maintain replicas in multiple geographic regions. 


US Users    -> US Replica
India Users -> India Replica
Europe Users -> EU Replica

Users access a nearby replica, reducing network latency and improving response time.

5. Increase Read Throughput

A single database can handle only a limited number of read requests.

For example: 


Single Database

Read Capacity = 10,000 requests/sec

Adding another replica increases total read capacity. 


Primary DB  = 10,000 reads/sec
Replica DB  = 10,000 reads/sec

Total Capacity = 20,000 reads/sec

This technique is heavily used in large-scale systems where reads greatly outnumber writes.

How Replication Works

The Primary Database accepts write operations.

Changes are then copied to replica databases. 


          Write
            |
            v
       Primary DB
        /   |   \
       /    |    \
      v     v     v
 Replica Replica Replica

All replicas contain the same data as the primary database.

Primary-Replica Architecture

This is the most common replication model.

Primary Database

  • Handles INSERT operations.
  • Handles UPDATE operations.
  • Handles DELETE operations.
  • Acts as source of truth.

Replica Database

  • Handles read operations.
  • Receives updates from primary.
  • Provides high availability.
  • Improves read scalability.

                Write
                  |
                  v
              Primary
                  |
        +---------+---------+
        |                   |
        v                   v
    Replica 1          Replica 2
        |                   |
      Read                Read

Real World Example

Consider an e-commerce application.

Users continuously browse products while relatively fewer users place orders. 


Read Requests  = 100,000/sec
Write Requests = 5,000/sec

If all requests hit a single database:

  • Database becomes overloaded.
  • Response times increase.
  • Application performance suffers.

Instead: 


                 Load Balancer
                        |
           +------------+------------+
           |            |            |
           v            v            v
       Replica1     Replica2     Replica3

                 ^
                 |
              Primary
              (Writes)

Reads are distributed among replicas while writes continue going to the primary database.

Replication vs Partitioning

Feature Replication Partitioning (Sharding)
Purpose Copy Data Split Data
Data Same Data Everywhere Different Data Per Shard
Improves Availability & Read Scaling Storage & Write Scaling
Example User table copied to all replicas Users split across shards

Replication + Partitioning

Large-scale systems usually combine both techniques.

Each shard maintains its own replicas. 


           Shard 1
         /    |    \
        R1   R2   R3

           Shard 2
         /    |    \
        R1   R2   R3

           Shard 3
         /    |    \
        R1   R2   R3

This provides:

  • Horizontal scaling.
  • High availability.
  • Read scalability.
  • Fault tolerance.

Advantages of Replication

  • High availability.
  • Eliminates single point of failure.
  • Improves read performance.
  • Supports disaster recovery.
  • Reduces latency by serving nearby users.
  • Improves fault tolerance.

Disadvantages of Replication

  • Additional infrastructure cost.
  • Replication lag may occur.
  • More complex architecture.
  • Data consistency becomes challenging.
  • Monitoring and maintenance overhead increases.

Quick Notes

  • Replication means maintaining multiple copies of the same data.
  • Primary database handles writes.
  • Replicas typically handle reads.
  • Main goals are availability, fault tolerance, and read scalability.
  • Replication improves read throughput but does not significantly improve write throughput.
  • Replication and partitioning solve different scaling problems.
  • Most modern distributed systems use both replication and partitioning together.

subtopics.name: Replication algorithms
subtopics.content:
Database Replication Strategies

Replication determines how data changes are propagated from one database node to other replica nodes.

Different replication strategies make different trade-offs between consistency, availability, latency, and fault tolerance.

1. Synchronous Replication

In synchronous replication, the primary database waits for replicas to acknowledge the write before confirming success to the client. 


Client
   |
 Write
   |
   v
Primary
   |
   +----> Replica 1 ✓
   |
   +----> Replica 2 ✓
   |
Success Returned

How It Works

  1. Client sends write request.
  2. Primary writes data.
  3. Replicas receive and store data.
  4. Replicas acknowledge success.
  5. Primary returns success to client.

Advantages

  • Strong consistency.
  • No data loss during failover.
  • All replicas remain synchronized.

Disadvantages

  • Higher write latency.
  • Slow replicas impact performance.
  • Lower availability.

Best Use Cases

  • Banking systems.
  • Financial transactions.
  • Critical business data.

2. Asynchronous Replication

In asynchronous replication, the primary acknowledges the client immediately after writing locally and replicates changes later. 


Client
   |
 Write
   |
   v
Primary
   |
Success Returned

Later...

Primary ---> Replica 1
Primary ---> Replica 2

How It Works

  1. Primary receives write.
  2. Data is written locally.
  3. Success returned immediately.
  4. Replication happens in background.

Advantages

  • Fast write performance.
  • Low latency.
  • High availability.
  • Scales well geographically.

Disadvantages

  • Possible data loss.
  • Replication lag.
  • Replicas may contain stale data.

Best Use Cases

  • Social media applications.
  • E-commerce systems.
  • Content platforms.

3. Semi-Synchronous Replication

Semi-synchronous replication is a hybrid approach.

The primary waits for acknowledgement from at least one replica before returning success. 


Client
   |
 Write
   |
Primary
   |
   +--> Replica 1 ✓
   |
Success Returned

Remaining replicas updated later.

Advantages

  • Better durability than asynchronous replication.
  • Lower latency than synchronous replication.
  • Balanced trade-off.

Disadvantages

  • More complex implementation.
  • Still possible to lose some data.

Best Use Cases

  • Enterprise applications.
  • Large SaaS platforms.

4. Leader-Follower Replication

Also known as Primary-Replica Replication.

Only one node accepts writes while replicas handle reads. 


            Writes
               |
               v
            Leader
          /    |    \
         /     |     \
        v      v      v
      F1      F2     F3

        Reads

Advantages

  • Simple architecture.
  • Easy conflict management.
  • Excellent read scaling.

Disadvantages

  • Leader becomes bottleneck.
  • Leader failure affects writes.

Examples

  • MySQL Replication.
  • PostgreSQL Streaming Replication.
  • Amazon RDS Read Replicas.

5. Multi-Master Replication

Multiple nodes can accept write operations simultaneously. 


      Master 1
         |
         |
      Master 2
         |
         |
      Master 3

All nodes can read and write.

Advantages

  • No single write bottleneck.
  • High availability.
  • Supports regional writes.

Disadvantages

  • Conflict resolution required.
  • Complex architecture.
  • Difficult consistency management.

Example Conflict


Master 1:
User Name = John

Master 2:
User Name = Johnny

Which value wins?

Examples

  • CouchDB.
  • Cassandra (multi-leader style).

6. Quorum-Based Replication

A write succeeds only when a minimum number of replicas acknowledge it.

Similarly, reads query multiple replicas and select the most recent version. 


N = 5 replicas

Write Quorum (W) = 3
Read Quorum (R) = 3

W + R > N

This ensures strong consistency.

Advantages

  • Strong consistency.
  • Fault tolerance.
  • No single point of failure.

Disadvantages

  • More network communication.
  • Complex implementation.
  • Higher latency.

Examples

  • Cassandra.
  • DynamoDB.
  • Riak.

7. Chain Replication

Replicas are arranged in a chain. 


Write

Head
 |
 v
Node 1
 |
 v
Node 2
 |
 v
Node 3
 |
 v
Tail

Read From Tail

Writes flow through every replica in sequence.

Advantages

  • Strong consistency.
  • High read throughput.
  • Predictable behavior.

Disadvantages

  • Higher write latency.
  • Failure handling is complex.

Examples

  • Microsoft Azure Storage (early versions).
  • Research distributed systems.

Comparison Table

Strategy Consistency Write Speed Complexity
Synchronous Very High Slow Medium
Asynchronous Low-Medium Fast Low
Semi-Synchronous High Medium Medium
Leader-Follower Depends Medium Low
Multi-Master Medium Fast High
Quorum High Medium High
Chain High Slow High

Quick Notes

  • Synchronous replication prioritizes consistency.
  • Asynchronous replication prioritizes performance.
  • Semi-synchronous provides a balance between the two.
  • Leader-Follower is the most commonly used replication architecture.
  • Multi-Master allows writes on multiple nodes but introduces conflicts.
  • Quorum replication is common in distributed databases.
  • Chain replication provides strong consistency with predictable ordering.

subtopics.name: Partition
subtopics.content:
Database Partitioning (Sharding)

Database Partitioning, also known as Sharding, is the process of splitting large datasets across multiple database servers.

Unlike replication, where the same data is copied to multiple servers, partitioning distributes different portions of data across multiple databases.

Why Do We Need Partitioning?

As applications grow, a single database eventually becomes a bottleneck.

Problems with a single database include:

  • Storage limitations.
  • High read and write traffic.
  • Slow query performance.
  • Expensive vertical scaling.

Partitioning solves these problems by distributing data across multiple servers.

Single Database Problem


               Application
                    |
                    v
               Database

Users: 100 Million
Orders: 500 Million
Products: 10 Million

Single Database becomes overloaded.

At some point adding more CPU and RAM is not enough.

What is Sharding?

Sharding divides data into smaller chunks called Shards.

Each shard stores only a subset of the total data. 


              Users Table

      +-----------------------+
      |     All Users         |
      +-----------------------+

                Split

     +---------+---------+---------+
     | Shard 1 | Shard 2 | Shard 3 |
     +---------+---------+---------+

Each shard is an independent database.

Benefits of Partitioning

1. Horizontal Scaling

Instead of upgrading a single server, new database servers can be added. 


DB1 + DB2 + DB3 + DB4

This allows the system to scale almost indefinitely.

2. Increased Write Throughput

Writes are distributed across multiple shards. 


Shard 1 -> 5,000 writes/sec
Shard 2 -> 5,000 writes/sec
Shard 3 -> 5,000 writes/sec

Total = 15,000 writes/sec

This is one of the biggest advantages over replication.

3. Reduced Query Load

Each database handles only a portion of the total data.

Queries become faster because the dataset is smaller.

4. Better Resource Utilization

Storage, CPU, and memory requirements are distributed across multiple servers.

Partitioning Strategies

1. Horizontal Partitioning

Rows are divided among multiple databases. 


Users Table

ID  Name
1   John
2   Mike
3   Sarah
4   Alex

Split Into:

Shard 1
---------
1 John
2 Mike

Shard 2
---------
3 Sarah
4 Alex

Advantages

  • Most common sharding approach.
  • Supports huge datasets.
  • Excellent scalability.

Disadvantages

  • Cross-shard queries become complex.
  • Requires shard routing logic.

2. Vertical Partitioning

Columns are divided among databases. 


User Table

ID | Name | Address | Photo

Split Into:

User DB
---------
ID | Name

Profile DB
---------
ID | Address | Photo

Advantages

  • Frequently accessed data remains lightweight.
  • Reduces unnecessary I/O.

Disadvantages

  • Requires joins across databases.
  • Increases application complexity.

Partitioning Algorithms

1. Range-Based Partitioning

Data is divided based on a range of values. 


User ID 1 - 1,000,000   -> Shard 1
User ID 1,000,001 - 2,000,000 -> Shard 2
User ID 2,000,001 - 3,000,000 -> Shard 3

Advantages

  • Easy to understand.
  • Efficient range queries.
  • Simple implementation.

Disadvantages

  • Hotspot problem.
  • Uneven shard growth.

2. Hash-Based Partitioning

A hash function determines the shard. 


Shard = Hash(UserId) % NumberOfShards

Example: 


User 101 -> Shard 2
User 102 -> Shard 1
User 103 -> Shard 3

Advantages

  • Uniform data distribution.
  • Avoids hotspots.
  • Excellent load balancing.

Disadvantages

  • Range queries become difficult.
  • Adding shards requires rebalancing.

3. Directory-Based Partitioning

A lookup service maintains the mapping between data and shards. 


Lookup Table

User 1 -> Shard A
User 2 -> Shard C
User 3 -> Shard B

Application first checks the directory service before accessing data.

Advantages

  • Flexible shard placement.
  • Easy shard migration.
  • Supports custom routing.

Disadvantages

  • Directory service becomes critical.
  • Additional lookup latency.

4. Geo-Based Partitioning

Data is partitioned based on geographical location. 


India Users  -> India DB
US Users     -> US DB
Europe Users -> EU DB

Advantages

  • Lower latency.
  • Better regional compliance.
  • Improved user experience.

Disadvantages

  • Cross-region queries are difficult.
  • Global reporting becomes complex.

Partitioning vs Replication

Feature Partitioning Replication
Purpose Split Data Copy Data
Write Scaling Excellent Limited
Read Scaling Good Excellent
Storage Distributed Duplicated
Main Goal Scale Data Improve Availability

Replication + Sharding

Most large-scale systems use both replication and partitioning together. 


          Shard 1
       /     |     \
      R1    R2     R3

          Shard 2
       /     |     \
      R1    R2     R3

          Shard 3
       /     |     \
      R1    R2     R3

Each shard stores different data, while replicas store copies of shard data.

This provides:

  • High availability.
  • Read scalability.
  • Write scalability.
  • Fault tolerance.

Quick Notes

  • Partitioning (Sharding) splits data across multiple databases.
  • Replication copies the same data across multiple databases.
  • Sharding improves write scalability.
  • Replication improves read scalability and availability.
  • Range, Hash, Directory, and Geo-based are common partitioning strategies.
  • Hash partitioning is the most common approach in distributed databases.
  • Large systems like Instagram, Uber, Amazon, and Facebook use both replication and partitioning.

subtopics.name: CAP Theorem
subtopics.content:
CAP Theorem

CAP Theorem states that a distributed system can guarantee at most two of the following three properties simultaneously:

  • C - Consistency
  • A - Availability
  • P - Partition Tolerance

The theorem was proposed by Eric Brewer in 2000 and later formally proven by Seth Gilbert and Nancy Lynch.

Why Do We Need CAP Theorem?

In distributed systems, data is stored across multiple servers located in different regions. 


      Node A  <------>  Node B
          \              /
           \            /
            \          /
              Node C

Network failures can occur between nodes. When this happens, the system must make a choice between consistency and availability.

CAP theorem helps us understand these trade-offs.

C - Consistency

Consistency means every read receives the most recent write.

After data is updated, all users should see the same value.

Example


Initial Value = 100

User A updates:
Balance = 200

User B reads:
Balance = 200

No user should see stale data.

Advantages

  • Accurate data.
  • Predictable behavior.
  • Required for financial systems.

Disadvantages

  • Higher latency.
  • May sacrifice availability.

A - Availability

Availability means every request receives a response.

The response may not always contain the latest data.

Example


Node A updated:
Balance = 200

Node B still has:
Balance = 100

User reads from Node B

Response Returned = 100

The data is stale, but the system remains available.

Advantages

  • Fast responses.
  • Better user experience.
  • High uptime.

Disadvantages

  • Users may see outdated data.

P - Partition Tolerance

Partition Tolerance means the system continues functioning even if communication between nodes is interrupted.

Example


Node A  X  Node B

(X = Network Failure)

Even though the nodes cannot communicate, the system continues operating.

In modern distributed systems, network failures are unavoidable, so partition tolerance is considered mandatory.

Understanding Network Partition

Suppose we have two database replicas. 


        Client
           |
           v

      Node A

          X

      Node B

The connection between Node A and Node B is broken.

Now the system must decide:

  • Reject requests to maintain consistency.
  • Accept requests to maintain availability.

This is the core idea behind CAP Theorem.

CP System (Consistency + Partition Tolerance)

CP systems prioritize consistency during network partitions.

If replicas cannot synchronize, some requests may be rejected. 


Partition Occurs

Node A -> Accepts Requests
Node B -> Rejects Requests

Result:
Consistency Maintained
Availability Reduced

Advantages

  • Strong consistency.
  • No stale reads.
  • Reliable transactions.

Disadvantages

  • Reduced availability during failures.
  • Users may experience errors.

Examples

  • ZooKeeper
  • Etcd
  • HBase
  • MongoDB (majority writes)

AP System (Availability + Partition Tolerance)

AP systems prioritize availability during network partitions.

Requests continue to be served even if data becomes temporarily inconsistent. 


Partition Occurs

Node A -> Accept Requests
Node B -> Accept Requests

Result:
Availability Maintained
Consistency Delayed

Advantages

  • High availability.
  • System remains operational.
  • Better user experience.

Disadvantages

  • Stale reads possible.
  • Eventual consistency required.

Examples

  • Cassandra
  • DynamoDB
  • Riak
  • CouchDB

CA System (Consistency + Availability)

A true CA system is possible only when no network partition exists. 


Single Database Server

Consistency = Yes
Availability = Yes
Partition Tolerance = Not Applicable

Since network partitions are unavoidable in distributed systems, pure CA systems are rare.

Examples

  • Single-node MySQL
  • Single-node PostgreSQL
  • Traditional RDBMS

Most Important Point

In real distributed systems, Partition Tolerance is mandatory.

Therefore the actual choice is usually: 


CP vs AP

Not: 


CA vs CP vs AP

Real World Example

Banking System

A bank account cannot show different balances on different servers. 


Balance = ₹10,000

User Withdraws ₹5,000

Every server must show:
₹5,000

Consistency is more important than availability.

Banking systems generally prefer CP.

Social Media System

If a user likes a post, seeing the updated like count a few seconds later is acceptable. 


Likes = 1000

Server A = 1001
Server B = 1000

Eventually both become 1001

Availability is more important than strict consistency.

Social media systems often prefer AP.

Eventual Consistency

Eventual Consistency means all replicas will become consistent if no new updates occur. 


Time T1

Node A = 100
Node B = 90

Replication Happens

Time T2

Node A = 100
Node B = 100

This concept is commonly used in AP systems.

Comparison Table

Property CP AP CA
Consistency High Lower High
Availability Lower High High
Partition Tolerance Yes Yes No

Quick Notes

  • CAP = Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance.
  • A distributed system can guarantee only two out of three properties.
  • Partition Tolerance is mandatory in modern distributed systems.
  • The practical choice is usually CP vs AP.
  • CP systems sacrifice availability during partitions.
  • AP systems sacrifice consistency during partitions.
  • Eventual Consistency is common in AP systems.
  • Banking systems prefer CP.
  • Social media systems often prefer AP.

subtopics.name: Message Queues
subtopics.content:
Message Queues

A Message Queue is a communication mechanism that enables different services or applications to communicate asynchronously.

Instead of one service directly calling another service and waiting for a response, messages are placed into a queue and processed later by consumers.

Why Do We Need Message Queues?

In traditional synchronous communication, a service directly calls another service and waits for the response. 


Order Service
      |
      v
Payment Service
      |
      v
Response

If the Payment Service becomes slow or unavailable, the entire request is affected.

Message Queues help decouple services and improve system reliability.

Synchronous vs Asynchronous Communication

Synchronous Communication


Client
  |
  v
Order Service
  |
  v
Payment Service
  |
 Wait for Response

Problems

  • Tight coupling.
  • Higher latency.
  • Service dependency.
  • Reduced fault tolerance.

Asynchronous Communication


Order Service
      |
      v
 Message Queue
      |
      v
Payment Service

The producer does not wait for the consumer.

Core Components

Producer

The service that creates and sends messages. 


Order Service
      |
      v
   Queue

Queue

A temporary storage where messages wait until consumers process them. 


Queue

Message 1
Message 2
Message 3
Message 4

Consumer

The service that reads and processes messages from the queue. 


Queue
  |
  v
Email Service

How Message Queues Work

  1. Producer creates a message.
  2. Message is stored in the queue.
  3. Consumer reads the message.
  4. Consumer processes the message.
  5. Message is acknowledged and removed.

Producer
   |
   v
+--------+
| Queue  |
+--------+
   |
   v
Consumer

Real World Example

Suppose a user places an order on an e-commerce website.

Several actions must happen:

  • Payment Processing
  • Email Notification
  • Inventory Update
  • Analytics Tracking

Without Message Queue


Order Service
   |
   +--> Payment
   |
   +--> Email
   |
   +--> Inventory
   |
   +--> Analytics

The user must wait until all tasks complete.

With Message Queue


Order Service
      |
      v
 Order Queue
      |
      +--> Payment Service
      |
      +--> Email Service
      |
      +--> Inventory Service
      |
      +--> Analytics Service

Order Service immediately responds to the user while background processing continues.

Benefits of Message Queues

1. Decoupling

Producers and consumers operate independently.

Neither service needs to know implementation details of the other.

2. Scalability

Consumers can be scaled independently. 


Queue
  |
  +--> Consumer 1
  +--> Consumer 2
  +--> Consumer 3

Higher throughput can be achieved by adding more consumers.

3. Reliability

Messages remain in the queue until successfully processed.

Temporary consumer failures do not cause data loss.

4. Load Buffering

Queues absorb sudden traffic spikes. 


10,000 Requests

      |
      v

   Queue

      |
      v

100 Requests/sec Processing

The queue acts as a buffer between producers and consumers.

5. Fault Tolerance

If a consumer crashes, messages remain available for later processing.

Message Delivery Patterns

1. Point-to-Point (Queue)

One message is processed by only one consumer. 


Producer
    |
    v
 Queue
    |
    +--> Consumer 1
    |
    +--> Consumer 2

Message processed by one consumer only.

Use Cases

  • Order Processing
  • Payment Processing
  • Job Scheduling

2. Publish-Subscribe (Pub/Sub)

One message is delivered to multiple subscribers. 


Publisher
     |
     v
    Topic
   /  |  \
  /   |   \
 S1  S2  S3

Use Cases

  • Notifications
  • Analytics
  • Event Broadcasting

Message Acknowledgement

After processing a message, the consumer sends an acknowledgement (ACK). 


Consumer
    |
Process Message
    |
   ACK

Only after receiving ACK does the queue remove the message.

Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)

Some messages repeatedly fail processing.

Instead of retrying forever, such messages are moved to a Dead Letter Queue. 


Main Queue
     |
     v
 Processing Failed
     |
     v
     DLQ

Benefits

  • Prevents endless retries.
  • Allows debugging failed messages.
  • Improves system stability.

Message Ordering

Many queue systems process messages in FIFO order. 


Message 1
Message 2
Message 3

Processing:

1 -> 2 -> 3

However, distributed systems may sacrifice strict ordering for scalability.

Message Queue vs REST API

Feature REST API Message Queue
Communication Synchronous Asynchronous
Response Required Immediate Not Required
Coupling Higher Lower
Traffic Spikes Difficult Handles Well
Fault Tolerance Lower Higher

Popular Message Queue Technologies

Technology Primary Use Case
RabbitMQ Traditional Message Queuing
Apache Kafka Event Streaming
Amazon SQS Cloud Queue Service
Apache ActiveMQ Enterprise Messaging
Redis Streams Lightweight Event Streaming

When Should You Use a Message Queue?

  • Email Sending.
  • Order Processing.
  • Payment Processing.
  • Video Processing.
  • Notifications.
  • Background Jobs.
  • Microservice Communication.
  • Analytics Pipelines.

Quick Notes

  • Message Queues enable asynchronous communication.
  • Producer sends messages, Consumer processes them.
  • Queues improve scalability, reliability, and fault tolerance.
  • Point-to-Point delivers a message to one consumer.
  • Publish-Subscribe delivers a message to multiple consumers.
  • Dead Letter Queues store failed messages.
  • Queues act as buffers during traffic spikes.
  • RabbitMQ is optimized for messaging, Kafka for event streaming.

subtopics.name: FIFO
subtopics.content:
FIFO (First In First Out) Queue

FIFO means the first message inserted into the queue is the first message processed. 


Queue

Message A
Message B
Message C

Processing Order:

A -> B -> C

Advantages

  • Maintains message ordering.
  • Predictable processing.
  • Ideal for transaction workflows.

Disadvantages

  • Lower throughput.
  • Slow messages can block subsequent messages.
  • Harder to scale.

Use Cases

  • Bank transactions.
  • Order processing.
  • Inventory updates.

subtopics.name: PRIORITY QUEUE
subtopics.content:
Priority Queue

Messages are processed based on priority instead of insertion order. 


Message A -> Priority 2
Message B -> Priority 5
Message C -> Priority 1

Processing:

B -> A -> C

Advantages

  • Critical tasks processed first.
  • Better resource utilization.
  • Supports SLA-based systems.

Disadvantages

  • Low-priority messages may starve.
  • Ordering not guaranteed.
  • Additional processing overhead.

Use Cases

  • Emergency notifications.
  • Fraud detection.
  • Critical alerts.

subtopics.name: Pull based
subtopics.content:
Pull-Based Messaging

Consumers actively request messages from the queue whenever they are ready. 


Consumer
    |
Pull Request
    |
    v
 Queue
    |
Message Returned

Advantages

  • Consumer controls processing rate.
  • Prevents overload.
  • Good backpressure handling.

Disadvantages

  • Additional polling requests.
  • Higher latency.
  • Wasted requests when queue is empty.

Examples

  • Apache Kafka Consumers.
  • Amazon SQS.

subtopics.name: Push based
subtopics.content:
Push-Based Messaging

The queue automatically delivers messages to consumers. 


Queue
   |
   v
Consumer

Consumers do not explicitly request messages.

Advantages

  • Low latency.
  • Immediate delivery.
  • Fewer network requests.

Disadvantages

  • Consumer overload possible.
  • Backpressure management is difficult.
  • Less consumer control.

Examples

  • RabbitMQ Push Model.
  • Webhooks.

subtopics.name: PUB SUB
subtopics.content:
How Pub/Sub Works

  1. Publisher sends message to a Topic.
  2. Topic stores/distributes the message.
  3. All subscribers receive the message.

Publisher
    |
    v
 ProductCreated Event
    |
    v
      Topic
    /   |   \
   /    |    \
Email Analytics Inventory

How Pub/Sub Works

  1. Publisher sends message to a Topic.
  2. Topic stores/distributes the message.
  3. All subscribers receive the message.

Publisher
    |
    v
 ProductCreated Event
    |
    v
      Topic
    /   |   \
   /    |    \
Email Analytics Inventory

Components

Publisher

Produces messages.

Topic

Logical channel used for message distribution.

Subscriber

Receives messages from a topic.

Advantages

  • Loose coupling.
  • Easy scalability.
  • Multiple consumers can receive the same event.
  • Supports event-driven architecture.
  • Independent service deployment.
  • Improved fault isolation.

Disadvantages

  • Debugging becomes difficult.
  • Message ordering challenges.
  • Event duplication may occur.
  • Eventual consistency issues.
  • Complex monitoring.

Important Factors in Pub/Sub Systems

Should subscribers receive messages in the exact order they were published?

Order Created Order Paid Order Shipped

Should messages survive broker failures?

Broker Crash ↓ Messages Still Available

subtopics.name: Hardware Failures
subtopics.content:
Hardware Failures

Hardware failures occur when physical infrastructure components stop functioning correctly.

Unlike software bugs, hardware failures are often random and can happen without warning.

Why Are Hardware Failures Important?

Modern distributed systems consist of thousands of servers, disks, network devices, and power systems.

As infrastructure grows, hardware failures become inevitable rather than exceptional events.

A well-designed system assumes that hardware components will eventually fail.

Common Hardware Failures

1. Disk Failure

Storage devices may become corrupted or completely stop working. 


Server
  |
  +--> Disk Crash

Data loss can occur if replication or backups are not available.

2. Server Failure

An entire server may become unavailable due to hardware malfunction. 


Application Server

CPU Failure
Memory Failure
Motherboard Failure

3. Network Failure

Routers, switches, or network cables may fail. 


Server A  X  Server B

(X = Network Failure)

This can cause communication breakdowns between services.

4. Power Failure

Unexpected power outages can bring down entire racks or data centers.

5. Overheating

Excessive heat may damage CPUs, memory modules, or storage devices.

6. Database Hardware Failure

Database servers are particularly critical because they store persistent data.

How Systems Handle Hardware Failures

  • Replication
  • Redundant Servers
  • Load Balancers
  • Automatic Failover
  • Backups
  • Multi-Region Deployments

Quick Notes

  • Hardware failures are random and unavoidable.
  • Examples include disk crashes, server failures, and power outages.
  • Replication and redundancy are primary mitigation strategies.
  • Distributed systems are designed assuming hardware will fail.

subtopics.name: Software Failures
subtopics.content:
Software Failures

Software failures occur when an application behaves incorrectly due to bugs, incorrect logic, configuration issues, or deployment mistakes.

Software vs Hardware Failures

Unlike hardware failures, software failures are generally deterministic.

If a bug exists, the same input often produces the same failure repeatedly. 


Input X
   |
   v
Application Crash

Repeat Input X
   |
   v
Application Crash Again

Common Software Failures

1. Bad Code

Incorrect business logic or coding mistakes can cause application failures. 


Divide By Zero
Null Pointer Exception
Array Index Out Of Bounds

2. Unhandled Exceptions

Unexpected conditions can terminate application execution. 


Database Down
      |
      v
Unhandled Exception
      |
      v
Application Crash

3. Edge Cases

Developers often test common scenarios but miss rare situations. 


Empty Input
Large Payload
Unexpected Characters

4. Configuration Issues

Incorrect environment variables, URLs, or credentials may break systems.

5. Deployment Issues

A faulty release can introduce new bugs into production.

6. Merge Conflicts

Code integration mistakes can create unexpected application behavior.

7. Performance Issues

Memory leaks, thread exhaustion, and inefficient queries may cause system degradation.

How Systems Handle Software Failures

  • Exception Handling
  • Retries
  • Circuit Breakers
  • Canary Deployments
  • Blue-Green Deployments
  • Monitoring & Alerting
  • Automated Testing

Quick Notes

  • Software failures are often deterministic.
  • Bad code is the most common cause.
  • Deployment and configuration issues frequently cause outages.
  • Monitoring and automated testing help reduce software failures.

subtopics.name: Human Failures
subtopics.content:
Human Failures

Human failures occur when people accidentally perform actions that negatively impact the system.

Many large-scale outages in production systems are caused by human mistakes rather than hardware or software issues.

Why Human Failures Matter

Humans have administrative access, deployment permissions, and infrastructure control.

A single mistake can affect millions of users.

Common Human Failures

1. Incorrect Configuration Changes


Wrong Database URL
Wrong API Endpoint
Incorrect Security Rules

2. Accidental Data Deletion


DELETE FROM USERS;

Without proper safeguards, critical data may be lost.

3. Wrong Deployment

Deploying unstable code directly into production can cause outages.

4. Infrastructure Misconfiguration

Incorrect firewall, DNS, or load balancer settings may break applications.

5. Security Mistakes

Accidental exposure of credentials or sensitive data.

Characteristics of Human Failures

  • Unpredictable
  • Difficult to eliminate completely
  • Can impact entire systems
  • Often caused by operational mistakes

How Systems Reduce Human Failures

Code Reviews

Multiple engineers review changes before deployment.

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

Restrict critical operations to authorized personnel.

Backups

Recover from accidental deletions.

Canary Deployments

Release changes gradually to reduce risk.

Automation

Reduce manual operational tasks.

Monitoring & Alerts

Detect issues quickly after mistakes occur.

Quick Notes

  • Human errors are one of the leading causes of outages.
  • Examples include wrong deployments, accidental deletions, and configuration mistakes.
  • Automation reduces operational risk.
  • RBAC, reviews, and backups are essential safeguards.
  • Many production incidents involve human mistakes.

subtopics.name: Basics monitoring
subtopics.content:
Monitoring & Observability

Building a system is only the first step. The real challenge is ensuring the system continues to operate reliably in production.

Monitoring and Observability help engineers understand system health, detect failures, troubleshoot incidents, and improve reliability.

What is Monitoring?

Monitoring is the process of collecting and tracking predefined metrics to understand the health and performance of a system. 


Application
     |
     v
 Metrics
     |
     v
 Dashboard
     |
     v
 Alerts

Monitoring answers:

  • Is the system healthy?
  • Is any service down?
  • Are error rates increasing?
  • Are resources running out?

What is Observability?

Observability is the ability to understand what is happening inside a system by analyzing its outputs.

Observability helps answer unknown questions during incidents. 


Logs
Metrics
Traces

   |
   v

Root Cause Analysis

Why Monitoring and Observability Matter

  • Detect failures quickly.
  • Reduce downtime.
  • Improve user experience.
  • Perform Root Cause Analysis (RCA).
  • Monitor infrastructure health.
  • Track edge cases and failures.

Key Questions Monitoring Answers

  • Are there any errors?
  • Is every component working properly?
  • What is the health status of the system?
  • Are edge cases being captured?
  • Are alerts triggered when components fail?

Three Pillars of Observability

1. Metrics

Numerical measurements collected over time. 


CPU Usage
Memory Usage
Latency
Error Rate

2. Logs

Detailed records of events happening in the application. 


2026-06-08 10:00:01 User Login Success
2026-06-08 10:00:05 Payment Failed

3. Traces

Track a request as it travels through multiple services. 


API Gateway
     |
Order Service
     |
Payment Service
     |
Database

Alerting

Monitoring becomes useful only when alerts notify engineers about issues. 


CPU > 75%  -> Warning

CPU > 90%  -> Critical Alert

Quick Notes

  • Monitoring tracks known metrics.
  • Observability helps investigate unknown issues.
  • Three pillars: Metrics, Logs, Traces.
  • Alerts are critical for production systems.
  • Observability helps perform Root Cause Analysis (RCA).

subtopics.name: APImonitoring
subtopics.content:
API Monitoring

API Monitoring is the process of continuously tracking API performance, availability, reliability, and usage.

1. Throughput

Throughput measures how many requests an API can process in a given time period. 


Requests Processed = 10,000

Time = 1 Second

Throughput = 10,000 RPS

Why It Matters

  • Measures API capacity.
  • Helps identify scaling requirements.
  • Detects traffic spikes.

2. Error Codes

Monitoring API error responses helps identify application failures.

Common Error Categories

Code Meaning
2xx Success
4xx Client Error
5xx Server Error

Example


500 Internal Server Error

Rate increases from:

0.1% -> 10%

Alert Triggered

3. Health Checks

Health checks verify whether APIs and dependent services are functioning correctly. 


GET /health

Response:

{
  "status":"UP"
}

Health checks often validate:

  • Application Status
  • Database Connectivity
  • Cache Availability
  • Message Queue Connectivity

4. Latency

Latency measures the time taken by an API to respond. 


Request Sent

10:00:00.000

Response Received

10:00:00.150

Latency = 150 ms

Important Metrics

  • Average Latency
  • P50 Latency
  • P95 Latency
  • P99 Latency

P99 is often the most important production metric.

Google Golden Signals

  • Latency
  • Traffic (Throughput)
  • Errors
  • Saturation

Most API monitoring systems are built around these signals.

Quick Notes

  • Throughput measures requests per second.
  • Error rates indicate application health.
  • Health checks verify service availability.
  • Latency measures response time.
  • P95 and P99 are more useful than averages.
  • Google's Golden Signals are frequently asked in s.

subtopics.name: Hardware monitoring
subtopics.content:
Hardware Monitoring

Hardware Monitoring tracks the health and utilization of physical infrastructure resources.

The goal is to identify infrastructure issues before they cause outages.

1. CPU Usage

CPU monitoring measures processor utilization. 


CPU Usage = 75%

Typical Thresholds


75% -> Warning Alert

90% -> Critical Alert

High CPU may indicate:

  • Traffic spikes.
  • Inefficient code.
  • Resource exhaustion.

2. Memory Monitoring

Memory monitoring tracks RAM consumption. 


Memory Usage = 85%

High memory usage may cause:

  • Out Of Memory (OOM) Errors.
  • Application crashes.
  • Garbage Collection pressure.

3. Disk Monitoring

Disk monitoring tracks storage utilization and disk performance.

Metrics

  • Disk Usage
  • Disk Read IOPS
  • Disk Write IOPS
  • Disk Latency

Disk Usage = 95%

Alert Triggered

4. Network Monitoring

Network monitoring tracks communication between systems.

Metrics

  • Bandwidth Usage
  • Packet Loss
  • Latency
  • Network Throughput

Packet Loss > 5%

Alert Triggered

Why Hardware Monitoring Matters

  • Detect infrastructure failures.
  • Prevent resource exhaustion.
  • Improve system reliability.
  • Enable proactive scaling.
  • Reduce downtime.

Popular Monitoring Tools

  • Prometheus
  • Grafana
  • Datadog
  • New Relic
  • Zabbix
  • Nagios
  • CloudWatch

Quick Notes

  • CPU, Memory, Disk, and Network are the four primary hardware metrics.
  • High CPU may indicate scaling needs.
  • High memory usage can cause OOM failures.
  • Disk monitoring helps prevent storage exhaustion.
  • Network monitoring detects connectivity issues.
  • Hardware monitoring is a core part of fault tolerance.
